author: @Aldizerboer
text:
By the way, after watching this video I've tried it myself.

It works like a charm for warming , only it didn't have alcohol so I used two stroke mix, I can't recommend it... I needed to open the doors and windows of my shack to not suffocate.

I used the fire wool of a catalytic converter, the ceramics of a catalytic converter and put a stainless steel cooking pot with holes drilled in the side on top of it.

I used my FLIR thermal camera and when it started the pot was 22c and after an hour it was 74c.

Next time I'll use alcohol 🤣

author: @kirkwoodcharles2932
text:
WORTH TAKING A LOOK!
Robert you had mentioned that you need fuel, oxygen and heat to get this process to start.  So I was thinking why not use a Hydrogen Generator to get the process going.  As vaporization is already done with the Hydrogen Generator.  If you wanted to take it further to create usable heat from the Converter, without to much energy loss... Use a decent size Stirling Engine and use the heat to run it then connect to a small generator  If you dial it all in using the correct amount of fuel, heat and voltage/amperage in each step by making each part in the process with individual controls.  This makes fine tuning much easier.  By just adding water mixed with whatever combination of additives you use in your water, for the hydrogen generator.  Myself, I like using ideas brought to us by Robert in the past.  Such as using UREA and a Strong set of Magnets to produce the most amount of Hydrogen...  By using the least amount of energy, as I like to get the most out of each component I use in an energy project, by using what I call "Stacking".  To me "Stacking" is done by using the byproduct of each process to convert it into more energy. 
When Robert showed us how the inside of the VEVOR heater worked it became very interesting to me.  So I bought my own heater, I found that much of the heat being created was going out the exhaust.  The first thing that I did was run it through another heat exchanger and attempt to use all the heat possible out of the unit,  much in the same way a high efficiency furnace works, by the time the exhaust gets outside it is just warm to the touch. Allowing it to exit at a safer temperature.  I did this by connecting several aluminum engine cylinders and heads together,  to error on the safe side I wanted cast iron sleeves in them,  then I sealed them up tight.  I ran another 12v fan taken from an old car interior duct system.  I purchased a cheap steel welding cart online and mounted a battery on the back of it where the tanks would have gone for the welder and placed my VEVOR heater on top of the cart.  Setting it up with slip fit connections to the exhaust and intake for easy removal of the heater.  I insulated and isolated the chamber below the VEVOR heater with another shelf,  providing 4" air space from the area containing the secondary heat exchanger.  Using an old car fan I created duct work around it and pulled in air from inside my workshop.  It's like I'm using 2 heaters at the same time but not using any extra fuel. I just got the 12v to run the old heater fan, as well as the VEVOR heater. I used a squirrel cage design fan blade so the noise is minimal. I also used the fans original speed controls.  I suppose you could even add in a catalytic converter to place along the exhaust route. Before it leaves the cart before the secondary heat exchanger or after,  prior to the exhausts and its trip outside.  
Something to consider is "Stacking" when building a project requiring energy, get as much out of it as you can!  Thanks, Chuck
